title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS
body: Homeless due to codes department. I went out and spoke to our homeless most lost their home due to the the government. I had a small kitchen fire didn’t burn the floor or anything the day after Christmas they put a demolition sign on my home. I’m dealing with them now. I’m trying to sell my home, I want out asap. Don’t move here it’s sucks.

title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS
body: KC 'water' bill is a combined package including water. Included is at least water, sewer, and trash. I live in a different city where my water bill is only $30 on average. However, if I add my trash service and sewer bills, it's around $100. Not quite the average you mentioned, but there's only two of us in a small house and we have HE appliances. Our sewer cost is based on water usage, so if we use more water, we pay more for both water and sewer.
author: @jceggbert5

title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS

author: @waddestudio

title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS
body: The main problems here for me as just a normal poor guy working in a warehouse making 23$ per hour. 1. Its super easy to get jobs, BUT even with a good paying job its hard to find an affordable place to live. 1 bedroom apartment rent is over 1000$ and mortages are around 250k on Zillow atleast. Even 23$ an hour has me living check to check with zero savings and barely able to keep my bank account positive. And 2. Almost every stoplight intersection in the city has a couple homeless people begging for money and yelling at cars that drive by....most of which being meth heads cause we have multiple meth capital sub-cites within KC. So basically its hard to afford a home of any kind unless ur a doctor, lawyer, tech guy, etc that makes big time money. If ur a normal lower middle class peasent u are screwed
author: @sosleepy333

title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS
body: In the late 1990's, there was a microburst (an inverted tornado) that ripped through downtown KCMO, severely damaging lots of buildings. It took quite some time to remove debris, repair structures and replace all the glass.
author: @SugahShy

title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS
body:
I moved to KCK 7 years ago. People are not very friendly and dating sucks. It's hard as a single adult, but I haven't made a single friend here. The humidity is on par with Boston, so it can be suffocating at times. It's moye affordable than Colorado, but I can't wait to move back. Or probably anywhere but here actually.
If you think the people are welcoming and friendly in KC, it's probably because you're from the coast where people are notoriously rude. People here are nice, but they aren't friendly. There's a difference.
author: @paulmichaud3230

title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS
body: If you are moving here to KC, something you should know that no one tells you, is that in some areas (I'm not sure how wide spread it is) the dirt has a very high clay content. Why does this matter? If you buy a home, you should know that the ground swells and shrinks A LOT. Nearly all of the homes in my area have to have piers installed to keep the foundation from sinking and breaking. This is extremely expensive. All drive ways and sidewalks eventually have to be replaced and or mud jacked. Also, working in the yard is awful because the dirt is either literally hard as concrete or very sticky. I have yard only shoes that I have to use a wire brush to get the mud off. I never understood how bricks could be made from clay until I moved here. I had a small clump of dirt soaking in a bucket of water for maybe 10 minutes and still couldn't break it up with my hands. I will never again buy a house where there is clay and plan to move largely because of it.
author: @paulmichaud3230

title: DON'T Move To KANSAS CITY... UNLESS You Can HANDLE These 15 THINGS
body: I have worked for both FedEx and UPS in Kansas City in Westport plaza brookside South Kansas City all over and I have seen a plethora of the homeless that occupy those areas that entire time! I'm talking literally well over 12 years consistently doing the same thing with no intention of changing their situation!

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: Born and raised in KC. I've been going to Arthur Bryant's since 1963. I moved away from KC in '84 for my job but on my trips home I always go back to Bryant's. The BBQ scene in KC has definitely changed in the last 40 plus years. I cherish those times I had at Bryant's when Arthur was "on the pit" himself.  My most memorable moment at Bryant's was meeting and shaking hands with Satchel Paige after an Athletics game, very cool. Thanks for the video.
author: @Kevin_747

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: My mom was the very first Museum Director of both the Negro League Baseball Museum and the American Jazz Museum. Her name was Dr. Rowena Stewart and she was formally a museum curator for the Philadelphia African American museum and the Motown museum. She passed in 2015.  Arthur Bryant’s is one my favorite BBQ restaurants. Thanks for mentioning the museums.
author: @ClarenceJohnson-t8f

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: oh, one more thing.  Back in the day - when you were in line, you handed the guy your plate.  My dad taught me to hold a 5 dollar bill under the plate and when the guy took it, you got a pound of meat on your sandwich.  They haven't done that in years, probably when Berbiglia bought them.  But those were the days....
author: @rjunk12349

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: My life partner was born and raised on Arthur Bryant's. He passed two years ago. Thank you for bringing him back to me.
author: @RogerMillerInVA

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: It's not the given #1 in KC, but it's definitely among the top. Everyone has a different #1 and it's all subjective but they are great. I would say them and gates are the old school and created the game as we know it in KC so they deserve their props and it's cool to see all that they do. Henry Perry, from Memphis, started doing BBQ in 1908. Charlie Bryant and his brother Arthur Bryant began working at Perry's and then Arthur took it over in 1946. Also in 46 a former cook for Henry Perry named Arthur Pinkard partnered with George Gates to start Gates and Sons BBQ. Arthur Bryant's was acquired by MMD Acquisitions LLC around November of 23 and Gates is owned by Ollie Gates as far as I can tell. The heritage of the two institutions, and their connection with Henry Perry give them the clout to be the originators.
author: @theofficialtoxn

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: I was 5 yrs old the first time my dad took us into Bryant's.  We would ride the baseball bus downtown and get off in front of Bryants to get our order to go.  We'd walk up the hill a couple blocks then go into old Municipal Stadium and watch the Kansas City A's baseball games and the Kansas City Chiefs football games. I am 64 now.  To this day Bryant's is best!
author: @darrellhornick7764

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body:
I remember as a little girl going to Arthur Bryant’s and Arthur’s dog “cupcake “ would be there with him and we’d laugh at cupcake as he would slide all over the floor from the grease. Arthur was always so kind to his customers!
He’d often bring us extra trays of ribs and brisket 🤩🤗🥳
author: @mariannemcguff1296

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: My parents and I went on a BBQ tour (we are from KC so we know our bbq trust me)....I kid you not, the gentleman who did all of the prep and bbq'ing.....while in line....whispered to us "if you give me $20 bucks cash I'll give you my discount....walk to the right and right before you get to the register ....cough and I'll hand you the beef brisket sandwiches and run out the door" Each sandwich that he gave us cost literally around $20.....We had three.....Years later he was awarded best employee at the company on Facebook.......He screwed us over with some  side things like sauce and stuff....but....hilarious and such a great memory!  Priceless....This place is one of the GREATEST restaurants in the world (not just bbq but in general).....God bless them!  Great video btw!
author: @hayeshumphries311

title: Day in the Life of the #1 BBQ in Kansas City
body: I lived in KC years ago and have been to Arthur Bryant’s. It’s the real deal of real deals. Authentic. I remember the window sill being lined up with those bottles of “juice” just warming in the heat of the afternoon sun. Amazing

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body:
David goggins entering the room:

“From the time you take your first breath, you become eligible to die. ... Stay hard!"
author: @jrh0994

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I have been an endurance athlete/triathlete my entire life. My perfect Saturday was a 45-mile bike ride, followed by an 8-mile run with a 5 hours rest afterwards and then an afternoon masters swim chased with several pints at the La Jolla Brewing Co. I always thought that my activity would make me bulletproof, and I'd live to be 100. 2 years ago, after a vigorous masters swim in the lead lane with an ex-olympian, holding times that I would be proud of in my 20's and 30's,- I went home, had a sandwich, sat down at my desk and (with no prior history - family or otherwise) had a heart attack. They told me it was SCAD (Spontaneous Coronary Arterial Dissection) It was a super rare type of heart attack that effects women having a baby (90%) and endurance athletes (10% male). It floored me. I asked my Cardiologist "what caused this?" he said - "I don't know". I asked, "Will it happen again?"  he said - "I don't know". I now hang in the back, I am at about 65% of where I was 3 years ago, I take long walks and am enjoying the view from the middle/back of the pack. I'll be 60 next year, and I know my kids would like to see another 40 more.
author: @hsheean

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I run a marathon a month. I train doing 20 to 30 miles a week, not too much. No gym. I just love the marathon.
author: @runningkirkwa2934

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I'm an avid walker.  I love to walk.  Sometimes I even enjoy a nice run or some hill climbs.  The runners in my life don't understand.  They're into their best time.  Training for that next race.  We don't understand each other.  I walk to lower my stress and be happy.  I hope it does good things for my heart.
author: @seattlegrrlie

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I knew a guy who ran and biked like crazy and he dropped dead of a heart attack while riding his bike.
author: @Aleesa321

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: Wow. This blew my mind. Completely contrary to what conventional wisdom has fed us for the last 30 years. Thank you for sharing.
author: @mikaylaschroeder6629

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I’ve begun slow jogging and I love it. Going on 56 I’ve never participated in sports but have always been drawn to running. Bad knees forced me to find a happy medium and following the advice of my orthopedist I perform what I love “in moderation” and note significant gains in muscle and a drop in my blood pressure. This is sound advice.
author: @rosemarylivingston5364

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: 9 years late but that was one of the most informative TED talks I've listened to. Thank you very much Doc!
author: @nuriac2480

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: Started running in middle school and have been a runner all my life. Had afib episodes all my adult life and they started to get worse as I got more into the longer distances. I couldn't even bend over when running (stopping to tie a shoe) without triggering my afib. About two years ago, I took a year off running due to a knee injury and then, when I starteted back exercising, I started running shorter distances and alternating with biking. Haven't had an afib episode since. Just my experience.
author: @CSWRB

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body:
Summary of recommendations he showed from studies, run:

10 to 15 or 20 miles per week - 12:57
maximum 8 miles an hour = 7:30 pace - 13:17
2 to 3 or 5 days a week - 13:37, 13:48
1 to 2.5 hours a week - 13:48
author: @DarxusC

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: Great research! I'm one of those marathoners who doesn't want to hear it but I need to. I adore running. Longevity obviously matters much more though. Thanks a lot for the bad news!! Lol!
author: @cindyaadams

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: Less is more! An excellent talk from an expert. Very helpful for a 72 year old with high blood pressure, anxiety and depression. I can only walk and not too fast either. This is very encouraging. I'm just off for my afternoon circuit of the block.
author: @colintyler1405

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: Im 61 - I run 3 - 4 times a week - 4 miles each time - 9 minute mile pace. This vid is exactly what I wanted to hear.
author: @LPCLASSICAL

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: 67. Started very slowly two years ago. Always exercised daily but not running. Now I get minimum 2.5 miles and some days 4 miles. I know it doesn’t prevent heart disease but I love it. I don’t do extreme workouts. I feel very good for someone with 5 autoimmune diseases. I credit diet and exercise.
author: @suzannebrach2812

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I'm 37 now, and I've been running since I was 14.  Nowadays, I average 25 miles per week, but when I was in my teens and twenties, I used to run even more, like 35-45 miles a week.  Once I entered my 30s, I purposely reduced the time spent running to incorporate more of strength training programs, such as weights, HIIT, and pilates.  I've never ran a full marathon in my life - I never saw the point of it.  I ran a half marathon once out of curiosity, and never felt the need to run more races.  I feel and look pretty good now.  I think the amount of running I'm currently doing is especially necessary for my mental well-being.
author: @gracey5512

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body:
for those who don't have a spare 18 rambling minutes of time this is the conclusion from Dr O'Keefe's paper:
The take home message for most is to limit one’s vigorous exercise to 30– 50 min/day. If one really wants to do a marathon or full-distance triathlon etc, it may be best to do just one or a few and then proceed to safer and healthier exercise patterns. On the other hand, light or moderate intensity exercise does not present the dose-dependent risks associated with excessive endurance exercise. A routine of moderate physical activity will add life to your years, as well as years to your life. In contrast, running too fast, too far, and for too many years may speed one’s progress towards the finish line of life.
author: @DrGFS

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I've been running since 1958 and have run over 100 marathons and lots of other distance races. I've trained 20,000 or so miles in that time. This video has caused me to alter my racing and training to where I'm only running about 25 miles per week (down from 50 miles per week) and have given up racing anything over 10K. My usual run is about 30 mins/day 4 or 5 days/week. I do some short sprints of 200m in those runs however with walking recoveries until my heart rate gets back to 70% of max which is when I start the next 200m interval. I'm grateful for the heads up which this video provided and hope that I'm on the right track. Thanks Dr. O'Keefe!
author: @medliberty

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: Kenneth Cooper's "Aerobics" in 1968 was the first wave in the running craze. He cautioned in his  book, the benefits of running are many but if you run more than 3.2 miles you are doing it for reasons other than health
author: @edwardbogusz5291

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I really appreciate this video! I'm a new runner and feel the pressure to get the miles up but I'm more comfortable knowing the range where it's safest for me and my health. It takes so much pressure off when I'm surrounded by people running marathons and even longer races. Nothing against them. Such great research and info.
author: @paigeyleigh54

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I believe there is more to it. Isn't that why training is so important? You build up to condition your heart and body to safely handle these activities.
author: @guadaluperodriguez7898

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I think a reason many people train too hard is because of things like they want to "lose weight." So they run more than they should a day.  It should be a lifestyle, not a temporary thing. And there is no hurry when you make it a permanent lifestyle change as long as you make progress in reaching your goals.
author: @Billy23201

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body:
I'm a runner, personally I believe that life isn't all about how long we live. The meanings of life, the adventures in it, the accomplishments are what matter more.

Distance running gives me that sense of being alive and freedom. I don't need to live so long. What I want is though, to let life blossom before it wilts.
author: @Yu_Adventurer

title: Run for your life! At a comfortable pace, and not too far: James O'Keefe at TEDxUMKC
body: I ran a marathon back in the mid 2000s. My goal was simply to finish. Around mile 16 there was someone on the ground near an aid station. I had grabbed some water in the chaos and ran very close not seeing him at first ...His face looked so peaceful (very fit, just his running shorts, it was a hot day) as I passed him. I thought he had just passed out. When I looked back, they were doing chest compressions :( maybe it was dehydration or overhydration. But that experience taught me that very fit people can have heart issues and that experience stuck with me. 🙏🏻
author: @MsSheilaC

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: She's a good teacher. Very attentive, gives the kids roles in the class to feel important, walks them onto the bus and waves goodbye. How kind.
author: @ashleyrobinson4671

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body:
It's not just the lack of screens. It's screens and:
The class size is smaller
The students don't get to school until 9am so they're more rested and had time for breakfast
The teacher gets there just before 8am and has an hour to prepare, so she can get organized without being exhausted
She has control over her lesson structure and timing
She hasn't been interrupted 20 times by adults coming in or calling for something
I started teaching in the early 2000s and in that time I've seen start times move earlier and earlier and the day go longer and longer
author: @catherinecates2154

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: I started teaching in 1981 and retired in 2016.  I saw such a difference in the student's behavior over the years.
author: @sharonreeves9093

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: Her saying "I love my job, and I can't imagine doing anything different," makes me so sad when compared to education today. I was just like her, and I thought for sure I would hang in there until retirement, but I left in 2017 after only 6 years. Education today destroys teachers' mental health. I was in a situation where it was a choice between having a healthy mind for my kids or being abused by parents and administrators. I adored the students.
author: @bryanna4548

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: This wasn't my experience in 2020s as a teacher, it was my experience as a child. I joined the profession with memories.  I left with traumatic memories.
author: @nrose3313

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: This was hard work, interaction, and discipline in the classroom.  Students were quiet and well behaved. I was 9 years old in 1988. This was how school life was back then.
author: @mattreynolds4954

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: Young people would probably laugh at the work we did and think it was boring and dumb, but man I thank god every day that I got to grow up in the 80s and 90s. It was a different world all together.
author: @ericthiel4053

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: For everyone astonished by the attention span of these kids, the moral is clear. Get your kids OFF screens! As a child of the 80s and a current teacher, I IMPLORE you.
author: @kcm7134

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: I was in 4th grade in 1988 - we’re all about 45 years old now. I will always feel extremely fortunate to have attended school in the 80s and 90s ❤
author: @zms8092

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: I began my teaching career in 1988, and this is exactly how it was. My first school and classroom looked exactly like this. My first group of 1st graders are now 45 years old. So much has changed in that time, mainly the self-discipline and focus of the students. Thirty-eight years later, and I’m retiring for the second time, 30 years in the public schools and 8 more years at a private school. If you want to be a teacher, don’t let the bad press discourage you. Being a teacher has been a privilege and a pleasure; it’s been my life’s work. I don’t think it’s going to hit me until I hear the school bus pulling up at 6:30 AM in August. Now at 60 years old, it’s time for a new chapter.
author: @sylvia4008

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: This was how it was. I was in high school in 1988. Teachers had control of the classroom and we learned
author: @iteration-4u

title: A Day in the Life of a Teacher (1988)
body: Very confident teacher. Teachers now are scrutinized and disrespected all around and constantly afraid of being reprimanded for simply trying to do their job. Many are burned out.
author: @Angela-ep9pm

title: How to spend a perfect day in Kansas City, MO
body: I'm a local resident of Kansas I would say go to Joe's BBQ and then head down to the world war 1 museum. If you're a baseball person go down to the K it's a beautiful stadium 👍👍👍

title: How to spend a perfect day in Kansas City, MO
body: As someone who has lived in Kansas City their whole life, none of the beautiful scenery is edited. It's a really beautiful place full of both fine and performing arts. I see shows all the time all around Kansas City, and they have all been fantastic. I definitely recommend Kansas City as a more affordable getaway.

title: How to spend a perfect day in Kansas City, MO
body: Blue bird cafe for breakfast. You also have to go to the WW1 monument and U ion station. Crossroads for beer, and rivermarket for lunch.

body: Your trip to Kansas City is so sad, so much to do and this is what you chose to show.

body: I've lived here in South KC for about 15 years. My husband has been here his whole life. We go to the Jackstack in Martin City. LOVE it!
